summary: I originally come from Somalia, where I was born and raised. I lived there until the Somali Civil War broke out, which disrupted my plans to continue my education beyond eighth grade. The war caused so much suffering and displacement for many people, including my family. It was a difficult time that forced us to flee our homeland.

I grew up in Mogadishu, the capital city of Somalia. It was a place I knew well and loved before the civil war started. When I first arrived in Houston, Texas, I found the city beautiful and it reminded me a lot of Mogadishu. My early memories and childhood are deeply connected to that city.

The Somali Civil War changed everything for me and my family. It started when I was still young and caused widespread death, injuries, and displacement. Because of the war, I couldn’t continue to high school or university as I had planned. It forced us to flee Somalia and seek refuge in Kenya.

After fleeing Somalia because of the civil war, my family and I found safety in Kenya. We lived in refugee camps there for one year and eight months, which was a relief after the hardships we faced. It was from Kenya, specifically Nairobi, that we eventually traveled to the United States. Kenya was a critical place in our journey to safety.

entity: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
summary: The United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, or UNHCR, played a vital role in helping my family. After my father contacted my elder sister in the United States, she arranged a sponsorship for us through the UNHCR. Thanks to their support, we were able to receive immigrant visas and move to the USA. Their assistance was crucial during our resettlement process.

The United States became my new home after we fled Somalia and lived in Kenya. I arrived here through a sponsorship process and faced many challenges, especially with the language barrier. Houston, Texas was the first city I lived in, and although it reminded me of Mogadishu, adapting was difficult. Over time, I learned to navigate life here despite the initial struggles.

Nairobi was the city in Kenya from which we departed to the United States. While living in refugee camps in Kenya, Nairobi was the place where we boarded the airplane that took us to Houston, Texas. It marked the transition point from our life as refugees to starting anew in America.

Houston, Texas was the first city I lived in when I arrived in the United States. I found it very beautiful and it reminded me of my hometown, Mogadishu. However, my first experiences there were tough because I didn’t speak English and struggled with transportation and communication. Despite these challenges, Houston became the place where I began my new life.
